serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of spicy chicken garlic sausage pasta is approximately 350 g, comprising a full plate of spaghetti tossed with sliced chicken garlic sausage in spicy tomato sauce.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Pasta with meat sauce, cooked (SR Legacy) and Chicken sausage, cooked (Foundation Foods); values adapted by component estimation for spicy chicken sausage pasta
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for spicy chicken garlic sausage pasta exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or USDA FoodData Central. Values are estimated by component-weighted averaging of USDA SR Legacy entries: cooked spaghetti (FDC ID 20124), chicken sausage cooked (FDC ID 174608), tomato sauce (FDC ID 11549), and garlic (FDC ID 11215), weighted approximately 55% pasta, 30% chicken sausage, 10% tomato sauce, 5% garlic and oil. Confidence is medium.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — spaghetti, white, boiled (GI ~45–49); composite dish estimated using pasta as dominant glycemic driver
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for spicy chicken garlic sausage pasta. GI estimated at 45 based on white spaghetti boiled (GI 45–49, University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson et al. 2021). Pasta is the dominant glycemic carbohydrate contributor (~55% by weight). Protein and fat from chicken sausage and tomato sauce further attenuate postprandial glycemic response. GL per serving calculated as: (45 × 20.5 g carbohydrate per 100g × 350g serving) / 100 = 32.2 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~420 mg per 100g; ~1470 mg per serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish as a single 350 g serving provides approximately 1470 mg sodium, representing over 60% of the WHO-recommended daily limit of 2000 mg, primarily from chicken sausage and tomato sauce.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load (GL ~32 per serving) from refined white spaghetti
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume this dish in controlled portions, as the refined spaghetti base contributes a moderate-to-high glycemic load per serving (~32 g GL); substituting wholegrain pasta and reducing portion size to 200 g would meaningfully lower postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Processed chicken sausage contributes saturated fat and sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals managing cardiovascular disease should be cautious with this dish due to the processed chicken sausage contributing saturated fat (~1.6 g per 100g) and high sodium (~420 mg per 100g); choosing a lower-sodium sausage variant and reducing portion size is advisable.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Spicy Chicken Garlic Sausage Pasta is a fusion pasta dish served in Singapore, consisting of spaghetti tossed in a spicy tomato-based sauce with sliced chicken garlic sausage. The dish is inspired by Indian spice profiles adapted into a Western pasta format, reflecting Singapore's multicultural culinary landscape. The spaghetti is boiled al dente and then sautéed with garlic, chilli, and tomato sauce before being combined with pre-cooked chicken garlic sausage. The result is a savoury, mildly spicy dish with a tangy tomato base, tender pasta strands, and smoky, garlicky sausage pieces. Nutritionally, a standard 350 g restaurant serving provides approximately 588 kcal, 32 g protein, 20 g fat, and 72 g carbohydrate. The dish is high in sodium (~1470 mg per serving) due to the processed sausage and tomato sauce, making it a concern for individuals with hypertension. The glycemic load is moderate (~32 per serving) owing to the refined white spaghetti base, which is relevant for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. The dish is halal-certified in most Singapore restaurant contexts and is free from dairy. It is not suitable for vegetarians or vegans. Common allergens include gluten from the pasta and soy from the sausage. The dish is typically served as a main course at casual dining and food delivery outlets across Singapore.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for this specific dish was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values were estimated by component-weighted averaging of USDA SR Legacy and Foundation Foods entries for cooked spaghetti, chicken sausage, tomato sauce, and garlic. GI was estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database using white spaghetti as the primary glycemic driver. Confidence is medium due to the composite estimation approach.
notes: 1. The input food name 'Spicy Chicken Garlic Said Sausage Pasta' contains a likely OCR or transcription error ('Said' is not a standard culinary term); the canonical name has been corrected to 'Spicy Chicken Garlic Sausage Pasta'. 2. Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy or Foundation Foods entries used for component estimation. 3. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the component sources used. 4. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the component sources used. 5. Trans fat (trans_fat_g) is 0.0; confirmed negligible/trace in chicken sausage and pasta components per USDA FoodData Central. 6. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA component data and should be verified against a direct laboratory analysis or a Singapore-specific food composition entry if one becomes available. 7. The dish is tagged as Indian cuisine in the source data; however, it is more accurately described as an Indian-influenced Singaporean fusion dish, as pasta is not a traditional Indian food item. 8. Halal status is assumed based on the restaurant context (#Foodcoholic, Singapore) and the use of chicken sausage; formal halal certification should be verified with the restaurant. 9. GL per serving calculated as: GI (45) × carbohydrate_g per 100g (20.5) × serving size (350g) / 100 = 32.2 g.
